description: Containing or holding as much or as many as possible; having no empty space. 'Pheno' names the quality of completeness—vessels that cannot take more, lives that lack nothing essential, states of satisfaction.
sound_symbolism: The word fills completely. 'Phe' /ɸe/ begins with breath filling, space occupied. Then 'no' closes with nothing lacking, fullness complete.
grammatical_notes: Functions as an adjective: 'pheno lunei' (full moon). Opposite of empty. Related to 'lorua' (abundant) but emphasizes completeness rather than overflow.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Fullness of presence—when fully here, nothing is lacking. The present moment is always complete.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Full barns meant survival—the satisfaction of provision, the security of enough.
